relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: David searches the surviving house of Saul for someone through whom he can honor Jonathan's covenant.
Ziba is summoned from the former royal household and answers under the language of service.
Mephibosheth is first identified through his relationship and disability, though neither exhausts who he is.
The king locates him in Machir's household at Lo-debar.
Royal messengers remove Mephibosheth from the place where he has been living.
A survivor of the displaced dynasty approaches the new king with understandable fear.
David promises safety, land restoration, and continual table fellowship for Jonathan's sake.
Mephibosheth speaks of himself through the degrading vocabulary demanded by extreme power imbalance.
David transfers Saul's estate back to Jonathan's son through a royal decree.
Ziba's large household is commanded to sustain the land while Mephibosheth remains near the king.
The table offers belonging, but it also places the vulnerable heir under permanent royal observation.
Mica's birth confirms Mephibosheth's continuing household and future.
The chapter ends by holding royal inclusion and bodily disability together without making either his whole identity.

contemplative_note: Kindness becomes trustworthy when it restores stolen resources without demanding abasement. Mephibosheth is not a symbol of brokenness rescued by a superior king; he is an adult survivor, landholder, parent, and covenant partner navigating dangerous royal power.
